title: Ficus setifera
intro: Ficus setifera, commonly known as the Banyan fig, is a species of fig tree native to tropical Asia, characterized by its unique aerial roots and ability to grow as an epiphyte. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine, offering various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which are believed to aid in the treatment of digestive issues and respiratory problems. The bioactive constituents of Ficus setifera include flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Ficus setifera is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, coughs, and skin irritations.
taxonomy: Ficus setifera is classified within the family Moraceae, which is a group of plants commonly known as the mulberry family. This plant is further classified in the genus Ficus, a group of plants that includes various species of figs. The species Ficus setifera belongs to the order Rosales, which includes a wide range of flowering plants. Ficus setifera is also known to be a part of the subfamily Ficoideae, a subfamily of plants within the Moraceae family. The exact classification of Ficus setifera may vary depending on the source, but it is generally accepted as a member of the family Moraceae.

side_effects: Ficus setifera is not typically used as a medicinal plant, but it may cause allergic reactions in some individuals. The sap of the plant can cause skin irritation, redness, and itching in people who come into contact with it. In rare cases, the sap may also cause an allergic contact dermatitis. However, more research is needed to fully understand the potential side effects of Ficus setifera. It is essential to exercise caution when handling the plant to avoid any adverse reactions.
